Integrating such AI-powered tooling automatically into developers' daily workflows is perceived positively, with the tool's accuracy remaining a critical factor in shaping developer perception and adoption.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Auto-Diagnose, a novel tool leveraging LLMs to diagnose integration test failures in software systems, achieving 90.14% accuracy and positive developer feedback after deployment.
AI.motivation: Diagnosing failures in complex software integration tests is challenging due to the large volume, unstructured nature, and heterogeneity of logs, leading to time-consuming and difficult processes.
AI.method: The authors developed Auto-Diagnose, an LLM-powered tool that analyzes failure logs, extracts concise summaries, and integrates with Google's internal code review system (Critique) to streamline diagnostics.
AI.result: Auto-Diagnose achieved 90.14% accuracy in diagnosing root causes during manual evaluation on 71 failures, was deployed across 52,635 distinct failing tests, and garnered overwhelmingly positive feedback from developers.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are highly effective in diagnosing integration test failures. Their integration into workflows is positively received, with accuracy being a crucial factor for adoption and usability.

title: Empirical Evaluation of PDF Parsing and Chunking for Financial Question Answering with RAG
summary: PDF files are primarily intended for human reading rather than automated processing. In addition, the heterogeneous content of PDFs, such as text, tables, and images, poses significant challenges for parsing and information extraction. To address these difficulties, both practitioners and researchers are increasingly developing new methods, including the promising Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems to automated PDF processing. However, there is no comprehensive study investigating how different components and design choices affect the performance of a RAG system for understanding PDFs. In this paper, we propose such a study (1) by focusing on Question Answering, a specific language understanding task, and (2) by leveraging two benchmarks from the financial domain, including TableQuest, our newly generated, publicly available benchmark. We systematically examine multiple PDF parsers and chunking strategies (with varied overlap), along with their potential synergies in preserving document structure and ensuring answer correctness. Overall, our results offer practical guidelines for building robust RAG pipelines for PDF understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how different components and design choices in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems impact performance in automated PDF processing, focusing on Question Answering and benchmarks from the financial domain.
AI.motivation: PDFs are designed for human reading and pose challenges for automated processing due to heterogeneous content like text, tables, and images.
AI.method: The study investigates PDF parsers, chunking strategies, and their synergies, leveraging benchmarks including TableQuest, a publicly available dataset.
AI.result: The results demonstrate practical insights into creating effective RAG pipelines for PDF understanding.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide systematic guidelines to enhance RAG system design for automated PDF processing, especially in preserving document structure and answer accuracy.

title: ReefMapGS: Enabling Large-Scale Underwater Reconstruction by Closing the Loop Between Multimodal SLAM and Gaussian Splatting
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting is a powerful visual representation, providing high-quality and efficient 3D scene reconstruction, but it is crucially dependent on accurate camera poses typically obtained from computationally intensive processes like structure-from-motion that are unsuitable for field robot applications. However, in these domains, multimodal sensor data from acoustic, inertial, pressure, and visual sensors are available and suitable for pose-graph optimization-based SLAM methods that can estimate the vehicle's trajectory and thus our needed camera poses while providing uncertainty. We propose a 3DGS-based incremental reconstruction framework, ReefMapGS, that builds an initial model from a high certainty region and progressively expands to incorporate the whole scene. We reconstruct the scene incrementally by interleaving local tracking of new image observations with optimization of the underlying 3DGS scene. These refined poses are integrated back into the pose-graph to globally optimize the whole trajectory. We show COLMAP-free 3D reconstruction of two underwater reef sites with complex geometry as well as more accurate global pose estimation of our AUV over survey trajectories spanning up to 700 m.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ReefMapGS, a 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)-based incremental framework, enabling COLMAP-free underwater scene reconstruction by leveraging pose-graph optimization and multimodal sensor data for accurate camera poses.
AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting for high-quality 3D reconstruction is hindered by its dependency on computationally expensive pose estimation methods, making it unsuitable for field robotics. There is a need for methods that can utilize multimodal sensor data to accurately estimate camera poses for efficient 3D scene reconstruction.
AI.method: The paper proposes ReefMapGS, an incremental reconstruction framework that starts with a high-certainty model. The system interleaves local tracking of new observations and optimizes the 3D Gaussian Splatting scene, while iteratively refining and globally optimizing the pose-graph trajectory.
AI.result: The authors demonstrate successful reconstruction of two underwater reef sites characterized by complex geometries without using COLMAP. Their approach also achieves more accurate global pose estimation over survey trajectories spanning up to 700 meters.
AI.conclusion: ReefMapGS effectively incorporates multimodal sensor data to eliminate the need for traditional structure-from-motion methods, enabling efficient and accurate 3D underwater scene reconstruction for field robot applications.

title: DBGL: Decay-aware Bipartite Graph Learning for Irregular Medical Time Series Classification
summary: Irregular Medical Time Series play a critical role in the clinical domain to better understand the patient's condition. However, inherent irregularity arising from heterogeneous sampling rates, asynchronous observations, and variable gaps poses key challenges for reliable modeling. Existing methods often distort temporal sampling irregularity and missingness patterns while failing to capture variable decay irregularity, resulting in suboptimal representations. To address these limitations, we introduce DBGL, Decay-Aware Bipartite Graph Learning for Irregular Medical Time Series. DBGL first introduces a patient-variable bipartite graph that simultaneously captures irregular sampling patterns without artificial alignment and adaptively models variable relationships for temporal sampling irregularity modeling, enhancing representation learning. To model variable decay irregularity, DBGL designs a novel node-specific temporal decay encoding mechanism that captures each variable's decay rates based on sampling interval, yielding a more accurate and faithful representation of irregular temporal dynamics. We evaluate the performance of DBGL on four publicly available datasets, and the results show that DBGL outperforms all baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper presents DBGL, a novel approach to model irregular medical time series while addressing challenges like heterogeneous sampling rates and variable decay irregularity.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve the reliability and accuracy in modeling irregular medical time series, which are crucial for capturing patient conditions but are often hindered by irregular sampling, asynchronous observations, and gaps.
AI.method: The authors propose DBGL, a Decay-Aware Bipartite Graph Learning framework. It introduces a patient-variable bipartite graph for modeling irregular sampling without artificial alignment and designs node-specific temporal decay encoding to address variable decay irregularity.
AI.result: DBGL is evaluated on four public datasets and achieves performance superior to all baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: DBGL effectively models irregular medical time series by preserving irregularity and capturing complex variable relationships, making it a robust solution for clinical data representation.

title: A Bayesian Perspective on the Role of Epistemic Uncertainty for Delayed Generalization in In-Context Learning
summary: In-context learning enables transformers to adapt to new tasks from a few examples at inference time, while grokking highlights that this generalization can emerge abruptly only after prolonged training. We study task generalization and grokking in in-context learning using a Bayesian perspective, asking what enables the delayed transition from memorization to generalization. Concretely, we consider modular arithmetic tasks in which a transformer must infer a latent linear function solely from in-context examples and analyze how predictive uncertainty evolves during training. We combine approximate Bayesian techniques to estimate the posterior distribution and we study how uncertainty behaves across training and under changes in task diversity, context length, and context noise. We find that epistemic uncertainty collapses sharply when the model groks, making uncertainty a practical label-free diagnostic of generalization in transformers. Additionally, we provide theoretical support with a simplified Bayesian linear model, showing that asymptotically both delayed generalization and uncertainty peaks arise from the same underlying spectral mechanism, which links grokking time to uncertainty dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper examines the phenomenon of grokking and task generalization in in-context learning using a Bayesian perspective, exploring how predictive uncertainty changes during training and its relationship with generalization.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanisms behind delayed transitions from memorization to generalization (grokking) in in-context learning, and its relation to predictive uncertainty.
AI.method: The study uses modular arithmetic tasks for transformers, employing approximate Bayesian techniques to analyze predictive uncertainty alongside theoretical modeling with a Bayesian linear model.
AI.result: The research finds that epistemic uncertainty collapses sharply during grokking, serving as a diagnostic for generalization. Additionally, delayed generalization and uncertainty peaks stem from a common spectral mechanism.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the utility of predictive uncertainty as a label-free diagnostic for generalization and links delayed generalization to spectral dynamics in Bayesian models.

title: Fall Risk and Gait Analysis in Community-Dwelling Older Adults using World-Spaced 3D Human Mesh Recovery
comment: Work was accepted at Computer Vision for Biomechanics Workshop (CVBW) at CVPR 2026
summary: Gait assessment is a key clinical indicator of fall risk and overall health in older adults. However, standard clinical practice is largely limited to stopwatch-measured gait speed. We present a pipeline that leverages a 3D Human Mesh Recovery (HMR) model to extract gait parameters from recordings of older adults completing the Timed Up and Go (TUG) test. From videos recorded across different community centers, we extract and analyze spatiotemporal gait parameters, including step time, sit-to-stand duration, and step length. We found that video-derived step time was significantly correlated with IMU-based insole measurements. Using linear mixed effects models, we confirmed that shorter, more variable step lengths and longer sit-to-stand durations were predicted by higher self-rated fall risk and fear of falling. These findings demonstrate that our pipeline can enable accessible and ecologically valid gait analysis in community settings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a video-based pipeline using 3D Human Mesh Recovery (HMR) to analyze gait parameters for older adults performing the Timed Up and Go (TUG) test. It demonstrates high correlation with established methods and predicts fall risks based on gait analysis.
AI.motivation: Current gait assessment in clinical practice is limited to simplistic measures like stopwatch-measured gait speed, which aren't comprehensive enough to predict fall risks or overall health in older adults.
AI.method: The study uses a 3D Human Mesh Recovery (HMR) model to extract spatiotemporal gait parameters (e.g., step time, sit-to-stand duration) from videos of older adults taking the Timed Up and Go (TUG) test. These parameters were compared with insole-based IMU measurements and analyzed using linear mixed effects models.
AI.result: The pipeline's video-derived step time was strongly correlated with IMU insole measurements. Moreover, it accurately identified correlations between shorter, variable step lengths and longer sit-to-stand durations with higher fall risk and fear of falling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed video-based pipeline offers a practical and ecological way to perform detailed gait analyses in community-based, non-laboratory settings, contributing to better fall risk assessment for older adults.

title: Socrates Loss: Unifying Confidence Calibration and Classification by Leveraging the Unknown
summary: Deep neural networks, despite their high accuracy, often exhibit poor confidence calibration, limiting their reliability in high-stakes applications. Current ad-hoc confidence calibration methods attempt to fix this during training but face a fundamental trade-off: two-phase training methods achieve strong classification performance at the cost of training instability and poorer confidence calibration, while single-loss methods are stable but underperform in classification. This paper addresses and mitigates this stability-performance trade-off. We propose Socrates Loss, a novel, unified loss function that explicitly leverages uncertainty by incorporating an auxiliary unknown class, whose predictions directly influence the loss function and a dynamic uncertainty penalty. This unified objective allows the model to be optimized for both classification and confidence calibration simultaneously, without the instability of complex, scheduled losses. We provide theoretical guarantees that our method regularizes the model to prevent miscalibration and overfitting. Across four benchmark datasets and multiple architectures, our comprehensive experiments demonstrate that Socrates Loss consistently improves training stability while achieving more favorable accuracy-calibration trade-off, often converging faster than existing methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Socrates Loss, a unified loss function to simultaneously improve neural networks' classification performance and confidence calibration, addressing stability-performance trade-offs in training.
AI.motivation: Deep neural networks struggle with confidence calibration, which is crucial for reliable performance in critical applications. Existing methods cannot balance strong classification and stable training effectively.
AI.method: A novel loss function (Socrates Loss) is proposed, leveraging uncertainty through an auxiliary 'unknown' class and a dynamic uncertainty penalty to optimize classification and calibration simultaneously, avoiding complex staged training processes.
AI.result: Socrates Loss improves training stability and achieves a better accuracy-confidence calibration trade-off, showing faster convergence across datasets and architectures compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Socrates Loss provides a robust mechanism to enhance reliability in neural network applications by effectively balancing classification accuracy and confidence calibration without training complications.

title: A Periodic Space of Distributed Computing: Vision & Framework
summary: Advances in networking and computing technologies throughout the early decades of the 21st century have transformed long-standing dreams of pervasive communication and computation into reality. These technologies now form a rapidly evolving and increasingly complex global infrastructure that will underpin the next aspiration of computing: supporting intelligent systems with human-level or even superhuman capabilities. We examine how today's distributed computing landscape can evolve to meet the demands of future users, intelligent systems, and emerging application domains. We propose a "periodic framework" for characterizing the distributed computing landscape, inspired by the systematic structure and explanatory power of the "periodic table" in chemistry. This framework provides a structured way to describe, compare, and reason about the behaviors and design choices of different distributed computing solutions. Using this framework, we can identify patterns in key system properties, such as responsiveness and availability, across the distributed computing landscape. We also explain how the framework can help in predicting future trajectories in the field. Lastly, we synthesize insights from leading researchers worldwide regarding the desired properties, design principles, and implications of emerging areas in the forthcoming distributed computing landscape and in relation to the periodic framework. Together, these perspectives shed light on the considerations that will shape the distributed computing landscape underpinning future intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a periodic framework to analyze and predict trends in distributed computing systems, aiding future intelligent systems.
AI.motivation: To address the need for a structured means of understanding and forecasting the evolution of distributed computing systems as intelligent systems demand more from infrastructure.
AI.method: Introduced a periodic framework inspired by the periodic table in chemistry, to systematically classify, compare, and analyze distributed computing solutions and their properties like responsiveness and availability.
AI.result: Utilized the framework to identify system patterns, predict trajectories in distributed computing, and complemented this with insights from global experts on future trends.
AI.conclusion: The periodic framework provides a comprehensive way to interpret and forecast developments in distributed computing, facilitating the design of systems supporting advanced intelligent systems.

title: Identity as Attractor: Geometric Evidence for Persistent Agent Architecture in LLM Activation Space
summary: Large language models map semantically related prompts to similar internal representations -- a phenomenon interpretable as attractor-like dynamics. We ask whether the identity document of a persistent cognitive agent (its cognitive_core) exhibits analogous attractor-like behavior. We present a controlled experiment on Llama 3.1 8B Instruct, comparing hidden states of an original cognitive_core (Condition A), seven paraphrases (Condition B), and seven structurally matched controls (Condition C). Mean-pooled states at layers 8, 16, and 24 show that paraphrases converge to a tighter cluster than controls (Cohen's d > 1.88, p < 10^{-27}, Bonferroni-corrected). Replication on Gemma 2 9B confirms cross-architecture generalizability. Ablations suggest the effect is primarily semantic rather than structural, and that structural completeness appears necessary to reach the attractor region. An exploratory experiment shows that reading a scientific description of the agent shifts internal state toward the attractor -- closer than a sham preprint -- distinguishing knowing about an identity from operating as that identity. These results provide representational evidence that agent identity documents induce attractor-like geometry in LLM activation space.

AI.tldr: The study investigates attractor-like behavior in large language models (LLMs) when representing cognitive agent identities, revealing semantic clustering in internal states and cross-architecture generalizability.
AI.motivation: To understand if persistent agent identities in LLMs induce attractor-like dynamics resembling semantic convergence.
AI.method: Conducted a controlled experiment comparing hidden states of original, paraphrased, and matched control "cognitive_core" prompts across layers and LLM architectures, followed by ablation and exploratory experiments.
AI.result: Paraphrased prompts displayed tighter clustering in internal states compared to controls, with semantic effects confirmed cross-architecture and influenced by structural completeness.
AI.conclusion: Agent identities in LLMs exhibit attractor-like geometry in activation space, tying semantic representation to persistent cognitive constructs.

title: Programming Language Co-Usage Patterns on Stack Overflow: Analysis of the Developer Ecosystem
summary: Understanding how developers combine programming languages in practice reveals the hidden structure of the software ecosystem: which languages are used as complements, which define coherent technology stacks, and which bridge disparate communities. We present a three-phase empirical pipeline that mines Stack Overflow posts by hundreds of thousands of developers across 186 programming languages, applying FP-Growth frequent itemset mining, Latent Dirichlet Allocation topic modeling, and Louvain community detection on a weighted co-usage graph, with the goal of characterizing co-usage coupling, latent developer specializations, and macro-level ecosystem structure simultaneously from behavioral data. FP-Growth identifies tight coupling clusters such as shell/bash, Swift/Objective-C, and the C-family with lift values far exceeding what individual language popularity predicts. LDA produces 25 developer profiles including Apple-platform developers, scientific and hardware programmers, functional/academic programmers, and two distinct Unix scripting sub-profiles. Louvain partitions the language graph into three macro-communities: web/enterprise, Apple ecosystem, and systems/scientific, and identifies Java as the highest-degree hub connecting all three. All three methods independently converge on the same ecosystem structure, providing strong cross-method validation of the findings.

AI.tldr: This study explores how developers use programming languages together using data from Stack Overflow, combining FP-Growth, topic modeling, and community detection methods to provide insights into language co-usage, developer specialization, and ecosystem structures.
AI.motivation: To uncover hidden structures in the software ecosystem by analyzing how developers combine programming languages in practice, and understand language complementarity, technology stacks, and community interconnections.
AI.method: A three-phase pipeline: FP-Growth for frequent itemset mining to identify tightly coupled languages, LDA for topic modeling to determine developer profiles, and Louvain community detection to map macro-level ecosystem structures from a weighted co-usage graph.
AI.result: Findings include tightly coupled language clusters like shell/bash and Swift/Objective-C, 25 developer profiles such as Apple developers and functional programmers, and three major ecosystem communities (web/enterprise, Apple, systems/scientific) with Java as a central hub.
AI.conclusion: The convergence of results from all three methods validates the existence of meaningful structures in the software ecosystem, offering insights into developer behavior, language co-usage, and community segmentation.

title: Leveraging Weighted Syntactic and Semantic Context Assessment Summary (wSSAS) Towards Text Categorization Using LLMs
summary:
The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) for reliable, enterprise-grade analytics such as text categorization is often hindered by the stochastic nature of attention mechanisms and sensitivity to noise that compromise their analytical precision and reproducibility. To address these technical frictions, this paper introduces the Weighted Syntactic and Semantic Context Assessment Summary (wSSAS), a deterministic framework designed to enforce data integrity on large-scale, chaotic datasets. We propose a two-phased validation framework that first organizes raw text into a hierarchical classification structure containing Themes, Stories, and Clusters. It then leverages a Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) to prioritize high-value semantic features, ensuring the model's attention remains focused on the most representative data points. By incorporating this scoring mechanism into a Summary-of-Summaries (SoS) architecture, the framework effectively isolates essential information and mitigates background noise during data aggregation.
  Experimental results using Gemini 2.0 Flash Lite across diverse datasets - including Google Business reviews, Amazon Product reviews, and Goodreads Book reviews - demonstrate that wSSAS significantly improves clustering integrity and categorization accuracy. Our findings indicate that wSSAS reduces categorization entropy and provides a reproducible pathway for improving LLM based summaries based on a high-precision, deterministic process for large-scale text categorization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Weighted Syntactic and Semantic Context Assessment Summary (wSSAS), a deterministic framework aimed at enhancing the reliability of Large Language Models (LLMs) in text categorization tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs face challenges like stochastic attention mechanisms and sensitivity to noise, which compromise their analytical precision and reliability in enterprise-grade analytics.
AI.method: The wSSAS employs a two-phase framework. It organizes raw text into hierarchical classifications (Themes, Stories, and Clusters) and uses a Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) to focus attention on high-value semantic features. A Summary-of-Summaries (SoS) architecture is integrated to isolate essential information.
AI.result: Experiments using the Gemini 2.0 Flash Lite model on datasets such as Google Business reviews and Amazon Product reviews revealed wSSAS significantly enhances clustering and categorization accuracy while reducing entropy.
AI.conclusion: The wSSAS framework provides a deterministic, high-precision approach to improving text categorization and reproducibility in LLM analytics for large datasets.

title: A Foot Resistive Force Model for Legged Locomotion on Muddy Terrains
summary: Legged robots face significant challenges in moving and navigating on deformable and highly yielding terrain such as mud. We present a resistive force model for legged foot-mud interactions. The model captures rheological behaviors such as visco-elasticity, thixotropy of the mud suspension and retractive suction. One attractive property of this new model lies in its effective, uniform formulation to provide underlying physical interpretation and accurate resistive force predictions. We further take advantage of the resistive force model to design a new morphing robotic foot for effective and efficient legged locomotion. We conduct extensive experiments to validate the force model, and the results demonstrate that the morphing foot enhances not only the locomotion mobility but also energy-efficiency of walking in mud. The new resistive force model can be further used to develop data-driven simulation and locomotion control of legged robots on muddy terrains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a resistive force model for legged robots moving on muddy terrains, validated through experiments and showcasing an energy-efficient, mobility-enhancing robotic foot design.
AI.motivation: To address the difficulty legged robots face in navigating deformable, yielding terrains like mud and improve their locomotion effectiveness and energy efficiency.
AI.method: The study develops a resistive force model capturing complex rheological properties of mud and uses it to design a morphing robotic foot. Validation is done via extensive experiments.
AI.result: The experiments validate the resistive force model and demonstrate that the morphing foot improves both mobility and energy efficiency for robots on muddy terrain.
AI.conclusion: The resistive force model and morphing foot design enhance robot locomotion on deformable terrains, with potential for simulation and control advancements.

title: Disposition Distillation at Small Scale: A Three-Arc Negative Result
summary: We set out to train behavioral dispositions (self-verification, uncertainty acknowledgment, feedback integration) into small language models (0.6B to 2.3B effective parameters) through a four-stage all-MIT distillation pipeline, with follow-on experiments on inference-time attention-head interventions and a frozen-base confidence-gated sidecar. An internal draft reported +33.9-point MCAS and +15.3-point HumanEval gains on a Qwen3-0.6B student; a second-pass sanity check falsified both numbers before publication. The HumanEval delta was a truncation artifact (n_predict=512) that inverted to -8.0 points at n_predict=1024; the MCAS gain disappeared under apples-to-apples scoring. That falsification triggered three subsequent arcs. Across (1) SFT/DPO LoRA on three model families and two domains, (2) inference-time attention-head tempering on o_proj, and (3) a training-free frozen-base sidecar reading the final-token hidden state h_last, we find no operator that moves judge-measured disposition without damaging content or collapsing into stylistic mimicry. The failure is consistent across five models (Qwen3-0.6B, Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3.5-0.8B, Gemma 4 E2B, and SmolLM2-1.7B-Instruct). A within-distribution cross-validation pass (AUC=0.683) collapsed to chance on fresh prompts (AUC=0.516). We contribute a three-arc negative result with mechanism, a two-failure-mode taxonomy for linear h_last probes, and an honest falsification pipeline that converts the class of false positives we ourselves produced into publishable negatives. As an independent finding, Gemma 4 E2B exhibits near-complete confidence-correctness decoupling on the Chef domain (assertion asymmetry -0.009; the model asserts at 91% regardless of correctness).

AI.tldr: The paper reports failed attempts to train behavioral dispositions into small language models using various techniques, finding no method that improves dispositions without compromising model output.
AI.motivation: The authors aimed to integrate key behavioral dispositions (self-verification, uncertainty acknowledgment, feedback integration) into small language models, striving for both improved interpretability and reliability.
AI.method: The study used a 4-stage MIT distillation pipeline, followed by three experimental approaches: SFT/DPO LoRA optimization, inference-time attention-head tempering, and a training-free frozen-base confidence-gated sidecar.
AI.result: None of the examined methods consistently enhanced the behavioral dispositions without adverse effects on model content or inducing mimicry, as shown across multiple models and validation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight inherent challenges in modifying language model dispositions without undermining their functionality, contributing negative results, failure-mode taxonomy, and a robust falsification pipeline to the research community.

title: Causal Diffusion Models for Counterfactual Outcome Distributions in Longitudinal Data
summary: Predicting counterfactual outcomes in longitudinal data, where sequential treatment decisions heavily depend on evolving patient states, is critical yet notoriously challenging due to complex time-dependent confounding and inadequate uncertainty quantification in existing methods. We introduce the Causal Diffusion Model (CDM), the first denoising diffusion probabilistic approach explicitly designed to generate full probabilistic distributions of counterfactual outcomes under sequential interventions. CDM employs a novel residual denoising architecture with relational self-attention, capturing intricate temporal dependencies and multimodal outcome trajectories without requiring explicit adjustments (e.g., inverse-probability weighting or adversarial balancing) for confounding. In rigorous evaluation on a pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic tumor-growth simulator widely adopted in prior work, CDM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art longitudinal causal inference methods, achieving a 15-30% relative improvement in distributional accuracy (1-Wasserstein distance) while maintaining competitive or superior point-estimate accuracy (RMSE) under high-confounding regimes. By unifying uncertainty quantification and robust counterfactual prediction in complex, sequentially confounded settings, without tailored deconfounding, CDM offers a flexible, high-impact tool for decision support in medicine, policy evaluation, and other longitudinal domains.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Causal Diffusion Model (CDM), a novel probabilistic framework for predicting counterfactual outcomes in longitudinal data and demonstrates its superior performance in complex scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle with time-dependent confounding and inadequate uncertainty quantification in predicting counterfactual outcomes for sequential treatments in longitudinal data.
AI.method: The authors propose the Causal Diffusion Model (CDM), which uses a residual denoising architecture with relational self-attention, eliminating the need for explicit deconfounding techniques.
AI.result: Using a pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic tumor-growth simulator, CDM achieves a 15-30% relative improvement in distribution accuracy and competitive or superior performance in point-estimate accuracy under high-confounding scenarios.
AI.conclusion: CDM effectively unifies uncertainty quantification and robust counterfactual prediction, offering a powerful and versatile solution for decision-making across longitudinal domains.

title: INDOTABVQA: A Benchmark for Cross-Lingual Table Understanding in Bahasa Indonesia Documents
summary: We introduce INDOTABVQA, a benchmark for evaluating cross-lingual Table Visual Question Answering (VQA) on real-world document images in Bahasa Indonesia. The dataset comprises 1,593 document images across three visual styles (bordered, borderless, and colorful) with one or more than one tables, and 1,593 question-answer sets in four languages: Bahasa Indonesia, English, Hindi, and Arabic. This enables evaluation of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in both monolingual (Bahasa documents with Bahasa questions) and cross-lingual settings (Bahasa documents with questions in other languages). We benchmark leading open-source VLMs (Qwen2.5-VL, Gemma-3, LLaMA-3.2) and GPT-4o and reveal substantial performance gaps, particularly on structurally complex tables and in low-resource languages. Fine-tuning a compact 3B and LoRA-finetuned 7B model on our dataset yields 11.6% and 17.8% improvements in accuracy. Providing explicit table region coordinates as additional input further improves performance by 4-7%, demonstrating the value of Spatial priors for table-based reasoning. Our findings underscore the importance of language-diverse, domain-specific datasets and demonstrate that targeted fine-tuning can significantly enhance VLM performance on specialized document understanding tasks. INDOTABVQA provides a valuable resource for advancing research in cross-lingual, structure-aware document understanding, especially in underrepresented regions of the world. Full dataset can be accessed in huggingface at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/NusaBharat/INDOTABVQA}

AI.tldr: INDOTABVQA is a dataset for cross-lingual Table Visual Question Answering (VQA) in real-world document images, focusing on Indonesian language documents. It contains images with tables across different styles and multilingual questions.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of evaluating Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on cross-lingual and structurally complex document understanding tasks, especially in low-resource languages like Bahasa Indonesia.
AI.method: The authors created a benchmark dataset with 1,593 document images and multilingual question-answer pairs in four languages. They evaluated leading VLMs and fine-tuned models to improve their performance using spatial priors and compact model adjustments.
AI.result: Fine-tuning improved model performance by 11.6% to 17.8%, with an additional 4-7% gain when explicit table region coordinates were provided. Significant performance gaps were noted in complex tables and for low-resource languages.
AI.conclusion: INDOTABVQA highlights the need for language-diverse, domain-specific datasets for advancing VLM capabilities. The dataset and findings encourage targeted fine-tuning approaches for document understanding tasks in underrepresented regions.

title: Algorithmic Analysis of Dense Associative Memory: Finite-Size Guarantees and Adversarial Robustness
comment: 21 pages, 9 figures, Accepted in New Frontiers in Associative Memory workshop at ICLR 2026
summary:
Dense Associative Memory (DAM) generalizes Hopfield networks through higher-order interactions and achieves storage capacity that scales as $O(N^{n-1})$ under suitable pattern separation conditions. Existing dynamical analyses primarily study the thermodynamic limit $N\to\infty$ with randomly sampled patterns and therefore do not provide finite-size guarantees or explicit convergence rates.
  We develop an algorithmic analysis of DAM retrieval dynamics that yields finite-$N$ guarantees under explicit, verifiable pattern conditions. Under a separation assumption and a bounded-interference condition at high loading, we prove geometric convergence of asynchronous retrieval dynamics, which implies $O(\log N)$ convergence time once the trajectory enters the basin of attraction. We further establish adversarial robustness bounds expressed through an explicit margin condition that quantifies the number of corrupted bits tolerable per sweep, and derive capacity guarantees that scale as $Θ(N^{n-1})$ up to polylogarithmic factors in the worst case, while recovering the classical $Θ(N^{n-1})$ scaling for random pattern ensembles. Finally, we show that DAM retrieval dynamics admit a potential-game interpretation that ensures convergence to pure Nash equilibria under asynchronous updates.
  Complete proofs are provided in the appendices, together with preliminary experiments that illustrate the predicted convergence, robustness, and capacity scaling behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes dense associative memory (DAM) retrieval dynamics, providing finite-size guarantees and explicit convergence rates under specific conditions.
AI.motivation: To overcome the lack of finite-size performance guarantees and explicit convergence rates in existing analyses of DAM, especially under conditions of random pattern sampling in large systems.
AI.method: The paper employs an algorithmic analysis to study DAM retrieval dynamics, proving geometric convergence under a separation assumption and bounded-interference condition. It also explores adversarial robustness and potential-game interpretations of DAM dynamics.
AI.result: The authors demonstrate finite-$N$ guarantees, adversarial robustness bounds, and capacity scaling of $Θ(N^{n-1})$ (with polylog adjustments in the worst case). They also provide experimental validation of their theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: This work offers explicit and verifiable guarantees for DAM performance, emphasizing its convergence efficiency, robustness, and storage capacity scaling behavior, while enhancing the theoretical understanding of DAM dynamics.

title: Local-Splitter: A Measurement Study of Seven Tactics for Reducing Cloud LLM Token Usage on Coding-Agent Workloads
summary: We present a systematic measurement study of seven tactics for reducing cloud LLM token usage when a small local model can act as a triage layer in front of a frontier cloud model. The tactics are: (1) local routing, (2) prompt compression, (3) semantic caching, (4) local drafting with cloud review, (5) minimal-diff edits, (6) structured intent extraction, and (7) batching with vendor prompt caching. We implement all seven in an open-source shim that speaks both MCP and the OpenAI-compatible HTTP surface, supporting any local model via Ollama and any cloud model via an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. We evaluate each tactic individually, in pairs, and in a greedy-additive subset across four coding-agent workload classes (edit-heavy, explanation-heavy, general chat, RAG-heavy). We measure tokens saved, dollar cost, latency, and routing accuracy. Our headline finding is that T1 (local routing) combined with T2 (prompt compression) achieves 45-79% cloud token savings on edit-heavy and explanation-heavy workloads, while on RAG-heavy workloads the full tactic set including T4 (draft-review) achieves 51% savings. We observe that the optimal tactic subset is workload-dependent, which we believe is the most actionable finding for practitioners deploying coding agents today.

AI.tldr: This paper studies seven tactics to reduce cloud LLM token usage by employing a local model as a triage layer in front of cloud models, with some tactics saving 45-79% of tokens depending on workload.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to identify methods to reduce the reliance on cloud-based language models, which are costly and have high latency, by effectively using local models.
AI.method: The authors systematically measure seven token-reducing tactics using a local model and cloud endpoint across various workloads, implementing and evaluating them with an open-source framework.
AI.result: Key findings include that combining local routing and prompt compression saves 45-79% tokens for specific workloads, while the full tactic set achieves up to 51% savings for RAG-heavy tasks.
AI.conclusion: Optimal token-saving strategies depend on specific workload types, providing actionable insights for practitioners using coding agents.

title: A longitudinal health agent framework
summary: Although artificial intelligence (AI) agents are increasingly proposed to support potentially longitudinal health tasks, such as symptom management, behavior change, and patient support, most current implementations fall short of facilitating user intent and fostering accountability. This contrasts with prior work on supporting longitudinal needs, where follow-up, coherent reasoning, and sustained alignment with individuals' goals are critical for both effectiveness and safety. In this paper, we draw on established clinical and personal health informatics frameworks to define what it would mean to orchestrate longitudinal health interactions with AI agents. We propose a multi-layer framework and corresponding agent architecture that operationalizes adaptation, coherence, continuity, and agency across repeated interactions. Through representative use cases, we demonstrate how longitudinal agents can maintain meaningful engagement, adapt to evolving goals, and support safe, personalized decision-making over time. Our findings underscore both the promise and the complexity of designing systems capable of supporting health trajectories beyond isolated interactions, and we offer guidance for future research and development in multi-session, user-centered health AI.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework and agent architecture for designing AI systems to support sustained, user-centered longitudinal health tasks by addressing adaptation, coherence, continuity, and agency in interactions.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in current AI agent implementations, which fail to support longitudinal health tasks effectively by not facilitating user intent and accountability, unlike prior health frameworks.
AI.method: The authors present a multi-layer framework and agent architecture built upon clinical and health informatics principles, showcasing use cases to demonstrate meaningful, adaptive, and safe longitudinal agent interactions.
AI.result: Through representative scenarios, the framework demonstrates how AI agents can sustain engagement over multiple interactions, adapt to changing user goals, and ensure safe, personalized health decision-making.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the potential and complexity of designing AI systems for longitudinal health support and provides guidance for advancing research and development in user-centered health AI frameworks.

title: From Plan to Action: How Well Do Agents Follow the Plan?
summary: Agents aspire to eliminate the need for task-specific prompt crafting through autonomous reason-act-observe loops. Still, they are commonly instructed to follow a task-specific plan for guidance, e.g., to resolve software issues following phases for navigation, reproduction, patch, and validation. Unfortunately, it is unknown to what extent agents actually follow such instructed plans. Without such an analysis, determining the extent agents comply with a given plan, it is impossible to assess whether a solution was reached through correct strategic reasoning or through other means, e.g., data contamination or overfitting to a benchmark. This paper presents the first extensive, systematic analysis of plan compliance in programming agents, examining 16,991 trajectories from SWE-agent across four LLMs on SWE-bench Verified and SWE-bench Pro under eight plan variations. Without an explicit plan, agents fall back on workflows internalized during training, which are often incomplete, overfit, or inconsistently applied. Providing the standard plan improves issue resolution, and we observe that periodic plan reminders can mitigate plan violations and improve task success. A subpar plan hurts performance even more than no plan at all. Surprisingly, augmenting a plan with additional task-relevant phases in the early stage can degrade performance, particularly when these phases do not align with the model's internal problem-solving strategy. These findings highlight a research gap: fine-tuning paradigms that teach models to follow instructed plans, rather than encoding task-specific plans in them. This requires teaching models to reason and act adaptively, rather than memorizing workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes whether programming agents adhere to instructed task-specific plans, revealing inconsistencies and suggesting strategies for improving plan compliance.
AI.motivation: To understand the adherence of AI agents to instructed task-specific plans, which affects their reasoning and performance in problem-solving tasks.
AI.method: Analyzed 16,991 trajectories of programming agents using task-specific plans across various LLMs and plan variations, assessing compliance and performance.
AI.result: Found agents often defaulted to incomplete or inconsistent internal workflows when not given explicit plans. Task success improved with standard plans and periodic reminders, while misaligned or overly detailed plans hurt performance.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning should focus on teaching models to reason and act adaptively, rather than embedding rigid task-specific plans, to enhance plan following and problem-solving capabilities.

title: LoSA: Locality Aware Sparse Attention for Block-Wise Diffusion Language Models
summary: Block-wise diffusion language models (DLMs) generate multiple tokens in any order, offering a promising alternative to the autoregressive decoding pipeline. However, they still remain bottlenecked by memory-bound attention in long-context scenarios. Naive sparse attention fails on DLMs due to a KV Inflation problem, where different queries select different prefix positions, making the union of accessed KV pages large. To address this, we observe that between consecutive denoising steps, only a small fraction of active tokens exhibit significant hidden-state changes, while the majority of stable tokens remain nearly constant. Based on this insight, we propose LOSA (Locality-aware Sparse Attention), which reuses cached prefix-attention results for stable tokens and applies sparse attention only to active tokens. This substantially shrinks the number of KV indices that must be loaded, yielding both higher speedup and higher accuracy. Across multiple block-wise DLMs and benchmarks, LOSA preserves near-dense accuracy while significantly improving efficiency, achieving up to +9 points in average accuracy at aggressive sparsity levels while maintaining 1.54x lower attention density. It also achieves up to 4.14x attention speedup on RTX A6000 GPUs, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LOSA, an efficient attention mechanism for block-wise diffusion language models that improves speed and accuracy while addressing KV Inflation issues in sparse attention.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome inefficiencies caused by memory-bound attention in long-context DLMs, particularly addressing the KV Inflation problem that arises during sparse attention.
AI.method: The paper proposes LOSA (Locality-aware Sparse Attention), which minimizes memory overhead by reusing cached prefix-attention results for stable tokens and applying sparse attention only to active tokens.
AI.result: LOSA delivers better efficiency and accuracy across multiple block-wise DLMs, showing up to a 9-point accuracy improvement under high sparsity, 1.54x lower attention density, and up to 4.14x speedup on RTX A6000 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: LOSA effectively solves the KV Inflation problem in sparse attention, enabling faster and more accurate block-wise DLMs with reduced computational demand.

title: 3DRO: Lidar-level SE(3) Direct Radar Odometry Using a 2D Imaging Radar and a Gyroscope
summary: Recently, the robotics community has regained interest in radar-based perception and state estimation. A 2D imaging radar provides dense 360deg information about the environment. Despite the radar antenna's cone of emission and reception, the collected data is generally assumed to be limited to the plane orthogonal to the radar's spinning axis. Accordingly, most methods based on 2D imaging radars only perform SE(2) state estimation. This paper presents 3DRO, an extension of the SE(2) Direct Radar Odometry (DRO) framework to perform state estimation in SE(3). While still assuming planarity of the data through DRO's 2D velocity estimates, it integrates 3D gyroscope measurements over SO(3) to estimate SE(3) ego motion. While simple, this approach provides lidar-level odometry accuracy as demonstrated using 643km of data from the Boreas-RT dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 3DRO, an extension of radar odometry to estimate SE(3) motion, with lidar-level accuracy tested on 643km data.
AI.motivation: To extend radar-based odometry methods from SE(2) to SE(3) for improved 3D state estimation using 2D imaging radars.
AI.method: The proposed 3DRO integrates 3D gyroscope measurements with 2D radar velocity data to estimate SE(3) ego motion.
AI.result: Achieved lidar-level odometry accuracy, validated using 643km of the Boreas-RT dataset.
AI.conclusion: 3DRO effectively enhances the accuracy and dimensionality of existing radar odometry frameworks, making them more versatile.

title: Subcritical Signal Propagation at Initialization in Normalization-Free Transformers
comment: 10 pages main text; 33 pages total; 5 figures in the main text, 24 figures total; preprint
summary: We study signal propagation at initialization in transformers through the averaged partial Jacobian norm (APJN), a measure of gradient amplification across layers. We extend APJN analysis to transformers with bidirectional attention and permutation-symmetric input token configurations by deriving recurrence relations for activation statistics and APJNs across layers. Our theory predicts how attention modifies the asymptotic behavior of the APJN at large depth and matches APJNs measured in deep vision transformers. The criticality picture known from residual networks carries over to transformers: the pre-LayerNorm architecture exhibits power-law APJN growth, whereas transformers with LayerNorm replaced by elementwise $\tanh$-like nonlinearities have stretched-exponential APJN growth, indicating that the latter are subcritical. Applied to Dynamic Tanh (DyT) and Dynamic erf (Derf) transformers, the theory explains why these architectures can be more sensitive to initialization and optimization choices and require careful tuning for stable training.

AI.tldr: This study explores gradient amplification across layers in transformer models, analyzing the averaged partial Jacobian norm (APJN) and its behavior under different architectures and configurations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand gradient propagation in deep transformer models, particularly its variation with architectural choices like LayerNorm and activation functions, which can impact model training stability and performance.
AI.method: The authors derive recurrence relations for activation statistics and APJNs for different transformer configurations, focusing on bidirectional attention and symmetric input tokens. They extend analytical techniques known from residual networks to transformers.
AI.result: They find that pre-LayerNorm architecture shows power-law APJN growth, while architectures with nonlinearities like elementwise tanh display stretched-exponential growth. This explains disparities in stability and sensitivity to initialization and optimization in models like DyT and Derf.
AI.conclusion: The study provides theoretical insights into how transformer architecture and initialization choices influence gradient propagation stability, aiding in designing better training practices for models like DyT and Derf transformers.

title: Ultra-low-light computer vision using trained photon correlations
summary: Illumination using correlated photon sources has been established as an approach to allowing high-fidelity images to be reconstructed from noisy camera frames by taking advantage of the knowledge that signal photons are spatially correlated whereas detector clicks due to noise are uncorrelated. However, in computer-vision tasks, the goal is often not ultimately to reconstruct an image, but to make inferences about a scene -- such as what object is present. Here we show how correlated-photon illumination can be used to gain an advantage in a hybrid optical-electronic computer-vision pipeline for object recognition. We demonstrate correlation-aware training (CAT): end-to-end optimization of a trainable correlated-photon illumination source and a Transformer backend in a way that the Transformer can learn to benefit from the correlations, using a small number (<= 100) of shots. We show a classification accuracy enhancement of up to 15 percentage points over conventional, uncorrelated-illumination-based computer vision in ultra-low-light and noisy imaging conditions, as well as an improvement over using untrained correlated-photon illumination. Our work illustrates how specializing to a computer-vision task -- object recognition -- and training the pattern of photon correlations in conjunction with a digital backend allows us to push the limits of accuracy in highly photon-budget-constrained scenarios beyond existing methods focused on image reconstruction.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method to enhance object recognition accuracy under ultra-low-light and noisy conditions using a correlated-photon illumination source optimized through end-to-end training with a Transformer backend.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the fact that traditional correlated-photon illumination methods focus on image reconstruction from noisy data, whereas many computer-vision tasks prioritize scene inference, such as recognizing objects, rather than image reconstruction.
AI.method: The authors propose 'correlation-aware training' (CAT), a method where a trainable correlated-photon illumination source and a Transformer model are optimized together. This enables the Transformer to leverage spatial correlations in photons to improve recognition accuracy, even in challenging imaging conditions.
AI.result: The method achieves up to a 15% improvement in object classification accuracy compared to uncorrelated-illumination-based computer vision methods, especially in ultra-low-light and noisy scenarios, and outperforms untrained correlated-photon illumination methods.
AI.conclusion: Correlating photon illumination patterns, specialized for object recognition tasks and jointly trained with the digital back-end, enables significant performance gains in recognizing objects in severely photon-limited conditions, surpassing previous methods focused on image reconstruction or untrained illumination.

title: Three Birds, One Stone: Solving the Communication-Memory-Privacy Trilemma in LLM Fine-tuning Over Wireless Networks with Zeroth-Order Optimization
summary: Federated Learning (FL) offers a promising pathway for collaboratively fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) at the edge; however, this paradigm faces a critical bottleneck: the prohibitive communication and memory overheads incurred by exchanging high-dimensional gradients. Furthermore, recent studies reveal that user training data can still be recovered from these local gradients, undermining the core privacy promise of FL. In this paper, we address this trilemma of communication, memory, and privacy by proposing pAirZero, a novel framework that synergizes Zeroth-Order (ZO) optimization with Over-the-Air (OTA) computation. Uniquely, pAirZero enables resource-constrained devices to submit their local gradient with only bit-level communication loads while participating in federated fine-tuning of LLMs with inference-level memory costs. This approach not only eliminates the high memory requirements needed for LLM fine-tuning but also alleviates the strict synchronization requirements that plague conventional OTA methods. We further formulate a rigorous optimization model to adaptively determine the optimal transmit power and noise levels, ensuring consistent privacy protection regardless of channel conditions. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of pAirZero in enabling secure, efficient LLM fine-tuning over wireless networks, with only 25% peak memory cost on OPT-125M and communication load orders of magnitude lower than conventional methods.

AI.tldr: pAirZero is a framework that improves memory, communication, and privacy constraints in federated fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs), leveraging zeroth-order optimization and over-the-air computation.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiencies and privacy risks in federated learning of LLMs, specifically the high communication, memory overheads, and data privacy concerns from transmitted gradients.
AI.method: pAirZero uses Zeroth-Order optimization combined with Over-the-Air computation to reduce communication to a bit-level load and optimize memory use, while adaptively managing transmit power and noise for consistent privacy.
AI.result: Experimental results show pAirZero achieves only 25% of the peak memory cost on OPT-125M and significantly reduced communication loads compared to traditional federated learning methods.
AI.conclusion: pAirZero successfully mitigates critical federated learning bottlenecks, providing a scalable and privacy-preserving solution for fine-tuning LLMs on edge devices.

title: WiseOWL: A Methodology for Evaluating Ontological Descriptiveness and Semantic Correctness for Ontology Reuse and Ontology Recommendations
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures. Submitted to a conference
summary: The Semantic Web standardizes concept meaning for humans and machines, enabling machine-operable content and consistent interpretation that improves advanced analytics. Reusing ontologies speeds development and enforces consistency, yet selecting the optimal choice is challenging because authors lack systematic selection criteria and often rely on intuition that is difficult to justify, limiting reuse. To solve this, WiseOWL is proposed, a methodology with scoring and guidance to select ontologies for reuse. It scores four metrics: (i) Well-Described, measuring documentation coverage; (ii) Well-Defined, using state-of-the-art embeddings to assess label-definition alignment; (iii) Connection, capturing structural interconnectedness; and (iv) Hierarchical Breadth, reflecting hierarchical balance. WiseOWL outputs normalized 0-10 scores with actionable feedback. Implemented as a Streamlit app, it ingests OWL format, converts to RDF Turtle, and provides interactive visualizations. Evaluation across six ontologies, including the Plant Ontology (PO), Gene Ontology (GO), Semanticscience Integrated Ontology (SIO), Food Ontology (FoodON), Dublin Core (DC), and GoodRelations, demonstrates promising effectiveness.

AI.tldr: WiseOWL is proposed as a methodology for systematically scoring and selecting ontologies for reuse, addressing challenges such as lack of systematic criteria and reliance on intuition.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of ontology selection for reuse in Semantic Web applications, where authors often struggle due to lack of systematic criteria, resulting in limited reuse and inconsistent interpretations.
AI.method: WiseOWL introduces a scoring methodology based on four metrics—Well-Described (documentation quality), Well-Defined (label-definition alignment via embeddings), Connection (structural interconnectedness), and Hierarchical Breadth (hierarchical balance).
AI.result: Evaluation of WiseOWL across six diverse ontologies demonstrated its effectiveness in providing normalized scores and actionable insights for ontology selection.
AI.conclusion: WiseOWL provides a systematic toolset for ontology selection to improve reuse, offering interactive evaluation and actionable feedback, aligned with Semantic Web objectives.

title: Structural Anchors and Reasoning Fragility:Understanding CoT Robustness in LLM4Code
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting is widely used to elicit explicit reasoning from large language models for code (LLM4Code). However, its impact on robustness and the stability of reasoning trajectories under realistic input perturbations remains poorly understood. Prior work has largely evaluated CoT through final correctness, leaving a critical gap in understanding how CoT reshapes internal uncertainty dynamics and why it sometimes harms rather than helps code generation. We suggest that CoT is not uniformly beneficial; instead, its robustness depends on whether perturbations destabilize structurally sensitive commitment points along the reasoning-to-code trajectory. We conduct a controlled, large-scale empirical study of CoT across six models and two code benchmarks (MHPP and BigCodeBench), subjecting task docstrings to systematic character-, word-, and sentence-level perturbations. We instrument full generation traces with token-level uncertainty and define three novel structural anchors: reasoning-code transition, symbolic commitment, and algorithmic articulation. Findings: (1) CoT does not yield uniform performance or robustness gains: its benefits are contingent on model family, task structure, and prompt explicitness. (2) CoT and No-CoT exhibit distinct robustness profiles, with different perturbation families triggering different failure modes. (3) We identify three recurrent trajectory deformations--Lengthening, Branching, and Simplification--that systematically emerge when perturbations interact with structural anchors and explain failure patterns. (4) Early-stage uncertainty serves as a reliable diagnostic signal for localizing where trajectory instability begins around sensitive anchors. These results provide a unified explanation for CoT's mixed performance and suggest design principles for building more robust reasoning-based code generators.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the robustness and stability of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting in large language models for code (LLM4Code), revealing that CoT benefits depend on task, model, and prompt specifics while introducing vulnerabilities under input perturbations.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the lack of understanding regarding how CoT reshapes uncertainty dynamics and why it sometimes harms code generation, especially under realistic input perturbations.
AI.method: A large-scale empirical study across six models and two code benchmarks (MHPP and BigCodeBench) was conducted, examining CoT's behavior under systematic character-, word-, and sentence-level input perturbations and analyzing token-level uncertainty and structural anchors.
AI.result: CoT’s performance and robustness are non-uniform, exhibiting failure modes like Lengthening, Branching, and Simplification when perturbations interact with structurally sensitive points. Distinct robustness profiles emerge between CoT and No-CoT.
AI.conclusion: The mixed performance of CoT can be explained by its interactions with structural anchors and perturbations. Early uncertainty diagnostics and design principles targeting stability can improve reasoning-based code generators.

title: Robust Explanations for User Trust in Enterprise NLP Systems
summary: Robust explanations are increasingly required for user trust in enterprise NLP, yet pre-deployment validation is difficult in the common case of black-box deployment (API-only access) where representation-based explainers are infeasible and existing studies provide limited guidance on whether explanations remain stable under real user noise, especially when organizations migrate from encoder classifiers to decoder LLMs. To close this gap, we propose a unified black-box robustness evaluation framework for token-level explanations based on leave-one-out occlusion, and operationalize explanation robustness with top-token flip rate under realistic perturbations (swap, deletion, shuffling, and back-translation) at multiple severity levels. Using this protocol, we conduct a systematic cross-architecture comparison across three benchmark datasets and six models spanning encoder and decoder families (BERT, RoBERTa, Qwen 7B/14B, Llama 8B/70B; 64,800 cases). We find that decoder LLMs produce substantially more stable explanations than encoder baselines (73% lower flip rates on average), and that stability improves with model scale (44% gain from 7B to 70B). Finally, we relate robustness improvements to inference cost, yielding a practical cost-robustness tradeoff curve that supports model and explanation selection prior to deployment in compliance-sensitive applications.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework to evaluate the robustness of token-level explanations in NLP black-box settings and finds decoder LLMs outperform encoder-based models in explanation stability, especially with larger models.
AI.motivation: Black-box NLP deployments make it difficult to validate robust explanations for user trust, particularly under real-world noise, and require better evaluation frameworks, especially during shifts from encoder to decoder models.
AI.method: The authors introduced a black-box robustness evaluation framework based on leave-one-out occlusion and measured robustness via top-token flip rates under various perturbations (like swap, deletion, shuffling, back-translation) at multiple severity levels.
AI.result: Decoder LLMs were found significantly more stable (73% lower flip rates) compared to encoder models, with stability improving as model scale increased (e.g., 44% gains from 7B to 70B models).
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a practical evaluation framework and highlights the tradeoff between robustness and inference cost, assisting in model and explanation selection for sensitive-use cases.

title: Dynamic Modeling and Robust Gait Optimization of a Compliant Worm Robot
summary: Worm-inspired robots provide an effective locomotion strategy for constrained environments by combining cyclic body deformation with alternating anchoring. For compliant robots, however, the interaction between deformable anchoring structures and the environment makes predictive modeling and deployable gait optimization challenging. This paper presents an experimentally grounded modeling and optimization framework for a compliant worm robot capable of traversing corrugated pipes. First, a hybrid dynamic locomotion model is derived, in which the robot motion is represented by continuous dynamics within a corrugation groove and discrete switching of anchoring positions between adjacent grooves. A slack-aware actuation model is further introduced to map the commanded gait input to the realized body-length change, and an energy model is developed based on physics and calibrated with empirical power measurement. Based on these models, a multi-objective gait optimization problem is formulated to maximize average speed while minimizing average power. To reduce the fragility of nominal boundary-seeking solutions, a kinematic robustness margin is introduced into the anchoring-transition conditions, leading to a margin-based robust gait optimization framework. Experimental results show that the proposed framework captures the dominant locomotion and energy-consumption behavior of the robot over the tested conditions, and enables robust gait optimization for achieving speed-power trade-off.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a modeling and optimization framework for worm-inspired compliant robots that traverse corrugated pipes, addressing challenges in predictive modeling and deployable gait optimization.
AI.motivation: Understanding how worm-inspired locomotion can adapt to constrained environments like corrugated pipes and overcoming challenges related to interactions between deformable anchoring structures and environments.
AI.method: The authors develop hybrid dynamic models combining continuous dynamics and discrete anchoring transitions. They also introduce a slack-aware actuation model, energy model calibrated via empirical data, and a multi-objective optimization approach incorporating a robustness margin.
AI.result: Experiments confirm the framework effectively captures locomotion and energy consumption practices, while optimizing gait for speed-power trade-offs performs robustly.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables effective modeling and optimization for compliant worm robots, leading to improved performance in constrained environments like corrugated pipes.

title: Thermodynamic Liquid Manifold Networks: Physics-Bounded Deep Learning for Solar Forecasting in Autonomous Off-Grid Microgrids
summary: The stable operation of autonomous off-grid photovoltaic systems requires solar forecasting algorithms that respect atmospheric thermodynamics. Contemporary deep learning models consistently exhibit critical anomalies, primarily severe temporal phase lags during cloud transients and physically impossible nocturnal power generation. To resolve this divergence between data-driven modeling and deterministic celestial mechanics, this research introduces the Thermodynamic Liquid Manifold Network. The methodology projects 22 meteorological and geometric variables into a Koopman-linearized Riemannian manifold to systematically map complex climatic dynamics. The architecture integrates a Spectral Calibration unit and a multiplicative Thermodynamic Alpha-Gate. This system synthesizes real-time atmospheric opacity with theoretical clear-sky boundary models, structurally enforcing strict celestial geometry compliance. This completely neutralizes phantom nocturnal generation while maintaining zero-lag synchronization during rapid weather shifts. Validated against a rigorous five-year testing horizon in a severe semi-arid climate, the framework achieves an RMSE of 18.31 Wh/m2 and a Pearson correlation of 0.988. The model strictly maintains a zero-magnitude nocturnal error across all 1826 testing days and exhibits a sub-30-minute phase response during high-frequency optical transients. Comprising exactly 63,458 trainable parameters, this ultra-lightweight design establishes a robust, thermodynamically consistent standard for edge-deployable microgrid controllers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel solar forecasting algorithm using a Thermodynamic Liquid Manifold Network to resolve anomalies in autonomous off-grid photovoltaic systems, achieving high accuracy and physical compliance.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning models for autonomous solar forecasting display critical issues, like phase lags during cloud changes and impossible nocturnal power output, requiring a solution that respects atmospheric and celestial dynamics.
AI.method: The study uses a Thermodynamic Liquid Manifold Network to map meteorological and geometric variables onto a Koopman-linearized Riemannian manifold, incorporating Spectral Calibration and Thermodynamic Alpha-Gate for real-time forecasting.
AI.result: Validated over five years in a severe semi-arid climate, the model achieves an RMSE of 18.31 Wh/m², zero nocturnal error, and rapid weather response times, with 63,458 trainable parameters enabling edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: This ultra-lightweight network establishes a thermodynamically consistent, accurate, and efficient approach for improving autonomous photovoltaic system controllers.

title: Offline-Online Reinforcement Learning for Linear Mixture MDPs
summary: We study offline-online reinforcement learning in linear mixture Markov decision processes (MDPs) under environment shift. In the offline phase, data are collected by an unknown behavior policy and may come from a mismatched environment, while in the online phase the learner interacts with the target environment. We propose an algorithm that adaptively leverages offline data. When the offline data are informative, either due to sufficient coverage or small environment shift, the algorithm provably improves over purely online learning. When the offline data are uninformative, it safely ignores them and matches the online-only performance. We establish regret upper bounds that explicitly characterize when offline data are beneficial, together with nearly matching lower bounds. Numerical experiments further corroborate our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: This paper examines offline-online reinforcement learning in linear mixture MDPs under environment shifts, proposing an algorithm to utilize offline data adaptively based on its quality and relevance.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in reinforcement learning when offline data might be from a mismatched environment, while ensuring adaptive usage to balance learning efficiency and safety.
AI.method: The authors develop an algorithm that determines whether offline data should be leveraged during online learning, based on factors like data coverage and environment shifts. They also derive regret bounds and conduct numerical experiments.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm improves learning efficiency when offline data is informative and achieves online-only performance when offline data is uninformative. Theoretical bounds and experimental results support their findings.
AI.conclusion: Their approach successfully identifies when and how offline data should be incorporated, demonstrating advantages in adaptive reinforcement learning under environment shift.

title: The Second Challenge on Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection at NTIRE 2026: Methods and Results
summary: Cross-domain few-shot object detection (CD-FSOD) remains a challenging problem for existing object detectors and few-shot learning approaches, particularly when generalizing across distinct domains. As part of NTIRE 2026, we hosted the second CD-FSOD Challenge to systematically evaluate and promote progress in detecting objects in unseen target domains under limited annotation conditions. The challenge received strong community interest, with 128 registered participants and a total of 696 submissions. Among them, 31 teams actively participated, and 19 teams submitted valid final results. Participants explored a wide range of strategies, introducing innovative methods that push the performance frontier under both open-source and closed-source tracks. This report presents a detailed overview of the NTIRE 2026 CD-FSOD Challenge, including a summary of the submitted approaches and an analysis of the final results across all participating teams. Challenge Codes: https://github.com/ohMargin/NTIRE2026_CDFSOD.

AI.tldr: The NTIRE 2026 CD-FSOD Challenge evaluated methods for cross-domain few-shot object detection across distinct domains, receiving wide participation and innovative submissions.
AI.motivation: To systematically assess and promote advancements in generalizing object detection across unseen domains with minimal annotations.
AI.method: Organized a challenge attracting participants to propose and test methods for cross-domain few-shot detection under open-source and closed-source conditions.
AI.result: A total of 696 submissions were made by 31 active teams, 19 of which provided valid final results, offering a diverse range of strategies.
AI.conclusion: The challenge highlights state-of-the-art progress in CD-FSOD, encouraging innovation and collaboration within the research community.

title: Beyond Pre-Training: The Full Lifecycle of Foundation Models on HPC Systems
summary:
Large-scale pre-training of Foundational Models (FM) constitutes a computationally intensive first phase for enabling AI across diverse scientific and societal applications. This first phase has positioned High-Performance Computing (HPC) facilities as indispensable backbones of "Sovereign AI" initiatives. While the massive throughput requirements of FM pre-training align with the traditional capability-oriented mission of HPC, subsequent phases of the AI lifecycle, typically referred to as fine-tuning and inference, introduce operational paradigms that can conflict with established batch-processing environments. Moreover, these phases are not computationally trivial: they often require substantial high-end compute resources while exhibiting hardware utilization patterns that differ significantly from those of pre-training.
  This paper addresses the architectural and strategic challenges of operationalizing a complete AI lifecycle within a national supercomputing facility. We present a hybrid cloud-native platform being developed and deployed at the Swiss National Supercomputing Centre (CSCS) that combines diskless GPU-enabled HPE Cray EX compute nodes with virtualized commodity infrastructure. Orchestrated by Kubernetes, this novel service architecture bridges the gap between HPC batch processing and service-oriented workflows. We report our initial investigations into fine-tuning pipelines and highly available inference services, analyzing the associated trade-offs while improving user productivity. Our findings offer a blueprint for enabling supercomputers to integrate "AI Factories" services and workflows, supporting AI innovations into end-to-end scientific and industrial use cases.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses the development of a hybrid cloud-native platform at the Swiss National Supercomputing Centre to manage AI lifecycle operations. It addresses the integration of fine-tuning and inference workflows on HPC systems, orchestrated by Kubernetes.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to resolve architectural and strategic challenges associated with running the full AI lifecycle, including pre-training, fine-tuning, and inference, within national supercomputing facilities.
